content.text: Budgets

Azure budgets are threshold-based alert rules attached to a cost scope. When spending reaches or is forecast to reach a defined percentage of the budget limit, Azure sends email notifications and can trigger action groups. Budgets do not stop services — they report and can invoke automation that does so.

Budgets attach to scopes identical to Cost Analysis: subscription, resource group, management group, billing account, billing profile, or invoice section. A budget at a higher scope covers all child scopes.

attrs.alt: Azure budget alert lifecycle: definition, threshold crossing, notification, and action group automation

content.text: Budget Properties
Property
Values
Notes
amount
Positive number
Budget limit in the billing currency of the scope
timeGrain
Monthly, Quarterly, Annually, BillingMonth, BillingQuarter, BillingAnnual
How often the spend counter resets
timePeriod
startDate / endDate
Budget is inactive outside this window; use an end date far in the future for ongoing budgets
category
Cost / Usage
Cost is used for financial budgets; Usage is for quantity-based (e.g., compute hours)
notifications
Up to 5 threshold + type combinations per budget
Type: Actual (charges so far) or Forecasted (projected end-of-period)
contactEmails
List of email addresses
Receive the alert email directly; no action group needed
contactGroups
List of action group resource IDs
Trigger programmatic actions in addition to email
filter
Optional dimension / tag filter
Restrict the budget to specific services, resource groups, or tags
Creating Budgets via Azure CLI

# Monthly budget of $5,000 on a subscription with two thresholds
az consumption budget create \
  --budget-name "monthly-subscription-budget" \
  --amount 5000 \
  --time-grain Monthly \
  --start-date 2024-01-01 \
  --end-date 2024-12-31 \
  --scope "/subscriptions/<subscription-id>" \
  --notification-key "Actual_GT_80" \
    --notification-enabled true \
    --notification-operator GreaterThan \
    --notification-threshold 80 \
    --notification-threshold-type Actual \
    --notification-contact-emails finance@contoso.com cloudops@contoso.com

# List budgets on a subscription
az consumption budget list \
  --scope "/subscriptions/<subscription-id>" \
  --output table
Creating Budgets via the REST API
Budget Filters: Scope a Budget to a Tag or Service

Filters allow a budget to track only specific resources — for example, all resources tagged `env=production`, or only Virtual Machine charges. This enables per-team or per-workload budgets within a shared subscription.
Action Groups: Automating Budget Response

When a budget threshold is crossed, an action group can trigger an Azure Automation runbook, a Logic App, a Function, or a webhook. A common pattern is stopping non-production VMs when the budget alert fires, reducing spend automatically.
Creating an Action Group for Budget Response
Monitoring Budget Status Programmatically

import requests
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential

credential = DefaultAzureCredential()
token = credential.get_token("https://management.azure.com/.default").token
headers = {"Authorization": f"Bearer {token}"}

scope = "/subscriptions/<subscription-id>"
url = (
    f"https://management.azure.com{scope}/"
    f"providers/Microsoft.Consumption/budgets?api-version=2023-11-01"
)

resp = requests.get(url, headers=headers)
print(f"{'Budget':<40} {'Amount':>10} {'Spent':>10} {'Pct':>6} {'Status'}")
print("-" * 80)
for budget in resp.json().get("value", []):
    props = budget.get("properties", {})
    amount = props.get("amount", 0)
    spent = props.get("currentSpend", {}).get("amount", 0)
    pct = (spent / amount * 100) if amount else 0

    # Determine status
    if pct >= 100:
        status = "EXCEEDED"
    elif pct >= 90:
        status = "CRITICAL"
    elif pct >= 80:
        status = "WARNING"
    else:
        status = "OK"

    print(
        f"{budget['name']:<40} "
        f"{amount:>10,.0f} "
        f"{spent:>10,.2f} "
        f"{pct:>5.1f}% "
        f"{status}"
    )
Terraform: Provisioning Budgets as Code
Managing budgets in Terraform alongside subscriptions and resource groups ensures budget coverage is never skipped during infrastructure provisioning.
Budget Hierarchy: Organization-Wide Coverage

For enterprises with multiple subscriptions, budgets should be created at multiple levels. A management group budget catches total spend; per-subscription and per-resource-group budgets provide granular alerts. The following script provisions a consistent budget hierarchy across all subscriptions under a management group.

import requests
import time
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential

def provision_budget_hierarchy(
    management_group_name: str,
    mg_budget_amount: float,
    sub_budget_amount: float,
    alert_emails: list,
):
    credential = DefaultAzureCredential()
    token = credential.get_token("https://management.azure.com/.default").token
    headers = {
        "Authorization": f"Bearer {token}",
        "Content-Type": "application/json"
    }

    def make_budget_body(amount: float) -> dict:
        return {
            "properties": {
                "category": "Cost",
                "amount": amount,
                "timeGrain": "Monthly",
                "timePeriod": {
                    "startDate": "2024-01-01",
                    "endDate": "2099-12-31"
                },
                "notifications": {
                    "Actual_GT_80": {
                        "enabled": True,
                        "operator": "GreaterThan",
                        "threshold": 80,
                        "thresholdType": "Actual",
                        "contactEmails": alert_emails
                    },
                    "Forecasted_GT_100": {
                        "enabled": True,
                        "operator": "GreaterThan",
                        "threshold": 100,
                        "thresholdType": "Forecasted",
                        "contactEmails": alert_emails
                    }
                }
            }
        }

    def put_budget(scope: str, name: str, amount: float):
        url = (
            f"https://management.azure.com{scope}/"
            f"providers/Microsoft.Consumption/budgets/{name}"
            f"?api-version=2023-11-01"
        )
        resp = requests.put(url, headers=headers, json=make_budget_body(amount))
        print(f"  Budget [{resp.status_code}]: {scope}/{name}")
        return resp.status_code

    # 1. Management group budget
    mg_scope = f"/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/{management_group_name}"
    put_budget(mg_scope, "mg-total-budget", mg_budget_amount)

    # 2. List subscriptions under MG
    subs_url = (
        f"https://management.azure.com{mg_scope}/subscriptions"
        f"?api-version=2020-05-01"
    )
    subs = requests.get(subs_url, headers=headers).json().get("value", [])

    for sub in subs:
        sub_id = sub["name"]
        sub_scope = f"/subscriptions/{sub_id}"
        sub_display = sub.get("displayName", sub_id)
        print(f"Subscription: {sub_display}")
        put_budget(sub_scope, "monthly-subscription-budget", sub_budget_amount)
        time.sleep(0.5)  # avoid rate limiting


provision_budget_hierarchy(
    management_group_name="contoso-root",
    mg_budget_amount=50000,
    sub_budget_amount=5000,
    alert_emails=["finance@contoso.com", "cloudops@contoso.com"],
)
Budget Alert Troubleshooting
Budget alerts are sometimes delayed or miss thresholds. The following table covers the most common issues and their causes.
Issue
Cause
Resolution
Alert email not received
Email in junk/spam, or the email address was not verified when the budget was created
Check spam filters; ensure address is a valid mailbox, not a distribution list alias that blocks external senders
Budget shows 0% spent when costs exist
Budget scope is wrong — typically a subscription ID typo or a scope the caller lacks read access to
Verify the scope ARM path and that the caller has Cost Management Reader on that scope
Threshold crossed but no alert fired
Budget alert checks run ~every 8 hours — spend may have crossed and passed before the next check
Add a lower threshold (e.g., 70%) to catch trends earlier
Forecasted alert fires repeatedly
Forecasted thresholds re-evaluate each check cycle; once crossed, an alert fires on each cycle until reset
Treat forecasted alerts as informational; do not design automation that must fire exactly once per period
Budget does not reset next month
The budget endDate passed, or the startDate is in the future
Check timePeriod and ensure endDate is in the future (2099-12-31 for open-ended budgets)
currentSpend is 0 on a new budget
Cost data for a new scope may take 24–48 hours to populate after first resource creation
Wait one full billing day; verify the subscription has resources generating billable meters
Budget-Driven Automation: Stop VMs on Threshold
Action groups connected to budgets can trigger Azure Automation runbooks. The following runbook stops all VMs tagged `env=dev` when invoked — a common pattern for controlling dev environment costs.

# Azure Automation Runbook (PowerShell equivalent logic in Python for illustration)
# Deploy as a Python 3.8 runbook in Azure Automation

import os
import requests
from azure.identity import ManagedIdentityCredential

def stop_dev_vms(subscription_id: str, tag_key: str = "env", tag_value: str = "dev"):
    """
    Stop all VMs tagged with tag_key=tag_value in the subscription.
    Designed to run as an Azure Automation runbook triggered by a budget alert.
    """
    credential = ManagedIdentityCredential()
    token = credential.get_token("https://management.azure.com/.default").token
    headers = {
        "Authorization": f"Bearer {token}",
        "Content-Type": "application/json"
    }

    # Find running VMs with the target tag
    vms_url = (
        f"https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{subscription_id}/"
        f"providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines"
        f"?api-version=2024-03-01&$filter=tagName eq '{tag_key}' and tagValue eq '{tag_value}'"
    )
    resp = requests.get(vms_url, headers=headers)
    vms = resp.json().get("value", [])

    stopped = []
    for vm in vms:
        vm_id = vm["id"]
        vm_name = vm["name"]
        # Deallocate (stop and release compute resources = zero compute cost)
        deallocate_url = (
            f"https://management.azure.com{vm_id}/deallocate?api-version=2024-03-01"
        )
        stop_resp = requests.post(deallocate_url, headers=headers)
        print(f"Deallocate [{stop_resp.status_code}]: {vm_name}")
        if stop_resp.status_code in (200, 202):
            stopped.append(vm_name)

    print(f"\nStopped {len(stopped)} dev VMs: {', '.join(stopped) or 'none'}")
    return stopped

# Called automatically by Azure Automation when budget alert fires
stop_dev_vms(os.environ.get("AZURE_SUBSCRIPTION_ID", "<subscription-id>"))
Deallocate (not Stop) releases the compute allocation and eliminates VM compute charges. A stopped (but not deallocated) VM still charges for compute. For budget-driven automation, always deallocate.
Summary

Azure budgets are threshold alert rules scoped to any ARM or billing scope. They trigger email notifications and action groups when actual or forecasted spend crosses defined percentage thresholds. Up to 5 threshold/type combinations per budget allow graduated alerts (80% → warning, 100% → critical, 100% forecasted → pre-emptive). Filters on tags or service dimensions enable per-team budgets within a shared subscription. Budgets observe spend; action groups act on it.
Budgets do not stop services — they send alerts and can invoke action groups that run automation
Budget checks run approximately every 8 hours — there can be a delay between a threshold crossing and alert delivery
Use thresholdType: Forecasted for early warning before the month ends
Up to 5 notification objects per budget; each can have different threshold, type, and recipients
Budget currentSpend reflects the scope's cost as of the last cost data refresh (typically same-day lag)
